title: Anchoring on tool capability limit the mind
html:
It doesn’t matter how good a tool is; the bandwidth of the brain is always the delimiting plateau. You can buy a faster app, a sharper framework, a more elegant notation — and still hit the same ceiling, because the bottleneck was never the tool. It was the mind holding it.

Your mind is the ultimate tool. However, it can decay (needs repetition to maintain), and can be inconsistent (a side effect of plasticity — it morphs every time you use it, Hebb’s Law). These are real limitations, not flaws to be engineered away. But for all that, it remains the true-est form of your thought, because it is your thought. No external tool can represent an idea more faithfully than the mind that grew it.

So hold tools loosely. Treat every tool usage as an experiment rather than a commitment. Prepare to ditch it or evolve out of it the moment it stops serving the thinking underneath. A tool you cannot abandon has quietly become a cage.

The way out is to boil it down to first principles, to the core functions it actually performs. Then evaluate those against your own personal workflows — not the marketing, not what the tool could do, but what you are actually needed it to do. Adopt the function; stay free of the tool. (A thread I first picked up in a friend’s thinking.)

backlinks.title: Perfect-tools syndrome
backlinks.context: Anchoring on tool capability limit the mind
